2FA.description: A sign-in method that requires two forms of verification.
2FA.example: Enable 2FA before joining the production GitHub organization.

A/B.description: An experiment that compares two variants to measure which performs better.
A/B.example: Run an A/B test before changing the signup page for everyone.

ACK.description: Used to confirm that a message, request, or update has been received.
ACK.example: ACK, I will review the PR after standup.

ACL.description: A list of permissions that controls who or what can access a resource.
ACL.example: Update the ACL before giving the service access to the bucket.

ADR.description: A short document that records an important technical decision and its context.
ADR.example: Write an ADR for the database migration strategy.

AI.description: Software techniques that perform tasks commonly associated with human intelligence.
AI.example: The team is evaluating AI-assisted support triage.

API.description: A contract that lets software systems communicate with each other.
API.example: The mobile app calls the payments API during checkout.

ARR.description: Recurring revenue normalized to a yearly value.
ARR.example: Expansion ARR increased after the enterprise launch.

ASAP.description: Used when something should be handled quickly.
ASAP.example: Please share the incident timeline ASAP.

AWS.description: A cloud computing platform commonly used to host infrastructure and services.
AWS.example: The staging environment runs on AWS.

B2B.description: A product, service, or process intended for business customers.
B2B.example: This onboarding flow is for our B2B customers.

B2C.description: A product, service, or process intended for individual consumers.
B2C.example: The B2C signup funnel needs fewer required fields.

BFF.description: A backend layer tailored to the needs of a specific frontend experience.
BFF.example: The mobile app uses a BFF to simplify API responses.

BI.description: Reporting and analysis used to understand business performance.
BI.example: The BI dashboard tracks weekly revenue.

CAC.description: The cost of acquiring a new customer.
CAC.example: CAC went down after the referral campaign launched.

CD.description: A software delivery practice where changes are kept ready to release.
CD.example: The CD pipeline packages every successful main branch build.

CDN.description: A distributed network that serves content from locations closer to users.
CDN.example: Static assets are served through the CDN.

CI.description: A development practice where automated builds and tests run after code changes.
CI.example: CI failed because one unit test is flaky.

CI/CD.description: A combined workflow for automatically testing, building, and preparing software for release.
CI/CD.example: The CI/CD workflow runs tests before deployment.

CLI.description: A text-based way to interact with a program from a terminal.
CLI.example: Use the CLI to install the FullForm Quick Action.

CODEOWNERS.description: A repository file that defines who should review changes to specific paths.
CODEOWNERS.example: Update CODEOWNERS so the platform team reviews infra changes.

CORS.description: A browser security mechanism that controls cross-origin web requests.
CORS.example: The frontend failed because the API CORS policy blocked localhost.

CPU.description: The main processor that executes instructions in a computer or server.
CPU.example: CPU usage spiked during the import job.

CRM.description: A system or process for managing customer and sales interactions.
CRM.example: Add the new lead to the CRM.

CRUD.description: The four common operations for managing stored data.
CRUD.example: The admin page needs CRUD support for glossary entries.

CSRF.description: A web attack where a user is tricked into submitting an unintended request.
CSRF.example: The form includes a CSRF token.

CSS.description: A language used to style web pages.
CSS.example: The button spacing is controlled in CSS.

CTR.description: The percentage of users who click a link, ad, or call to action.
CTR.example: The new subject line improved CTR.

CVE.description: A public identifier for a known cybersecurity vulnerability.
CVE.example: Upgrade the dependency because it has a high-severity CVE.

DB.description: A system or store used to persist structured data.
DB.example: The user profile is saved in the DB.

DAU.description: The number of unique users active during a day.
DAU.example: DAU dropped after the login regression.

DDL.description: SQL statements used to define or change database schema.
DDL.example: The migration uses DDL to add a new index.

DML.description: SQL statements used to read or modify stored data.
DML.example: The cleanup script runs DML against archived records.

DNS.description: The internet system that maps domain names to network addresses.
DNS.example: Check DNS if the new domain is not resolving.

DORA.description: A set of delivery performance metrics often used by software teams.
DORA.example: The team tracks DORA metrics for release health.

DPA.description: A contract that defines how personal data is processed and protected.
DPA.example: Legal needs the vendor DPA before we enable the integration.

DPO.description: A role responsible for advising on and monitoring data protection practices.
DPO.example: Ask the DPO before exporting customer data.

DRI.description: The person accountable for driving a task or decision to completion.
DRI.example: Who is the DRI for the launch checklist?

DX.description: The experience developers have while using tools, APIs, or workflows.
DX.example: Better error messages would improve DX.

E2E.description: Testing or behavior that covers a complete user flow from start to finish.
E2E.example: The E2E test covers login, checkout, and receipt generation.

EC2.description: An AWS service for running virtual servers.
EC2.example: The worker runs on an EC2 instance.

EOD.description: Usually means before the end of the current workday.
EOD.example: Please send the deployment status by EOD.

ERD.description: A diagram that shows data entities and their relationships.
ERD.example: Add an ERD to the data model proposal.

ETA.description: An estimate for when something will be ready or completed.
ETA.example: What is the ETA for the bug fix?

ETL.description: A data pipeline pattern for moving and preparing data between systems.
ETL.example: The nightly ETL job populates the reporting tables.

FTE.description: A full-time worker or a staffing capacity measurement.
FTE.example: The project needs one more FTE next quarter.

FYI.description: Used to share information that may not require action.
FYI.example: FYI, the release branch was cut this morning.

GA.description: A release stage where a product or feature is broadly available.
GA.example: The API will reach GA next month.

GCP.description: A cloud computing platform commonly used to host infrastructure and services.
GCP.example: The analytics pipeline runs on GCP.

GDPR.description: A European privacy regulation for personal data processing.
GDPR.example: Delete user data according to GDPR requirements.

GFM.description: GitHub's Markdown variant with extensions such as tables and task lists.
GFM.example: The README uses GFM task lists.

GTM.description: The plan for launching and selling a product or feature.
GTM.example: Marketing is preparing the GTM plan.

GPU.description: A processor optimized for graphics and parallel computation workloads.
GPU.example: The model training job needs a GPU instance.

HLD.description: A design document that explains system behavior and architecture at a broad level.
HLD.example: Review the HLD before we start the detailed API design.

HTML.description: The markup language used to structure web pages.
HTML.example: The email template uses basic HTML.

HTTP.description: A protocol used for transferring resources on the web.
HTTP.example: The API returned an HTTP 404 response.

I18N.description: Designing software so it can support multiple languages and regions.
I18N.example: The settings page needs I18N support before launch.

IAM.description: Systems and policies that control identities and permissions.
IAM.example: Use IAM roles instead of long-lived access keys.

ICP.description: A description of the customer type a product is best suited for.
ICP.example: The sales team refined the ICP for enterprise accounts.

IDE.description: A software application for writing, running, and debugging code.
IDE.example: Open the project in your IDE.

IDP.description: A service that verifies user identity for authentication flows.
IDP.example: Configure the company IDP before enabling SSO.

IP.description: A network protocol used to address and route data between devices.
IP.example: Allowlist the office IP before testing the staging service.

IOPS.description: A storage performance metric for read and write operations.
IOPS.example: The database volume needs higher IOPS.

IRL.description: Used to distinguish offline or in-person context from online discussion.

JSON.description: A lightweight text format for structured data exchange.
JSON.example: The glossary is stored as JSON.

JWT.description: A compact token format commonly used to pass identity or authorization claims.
JWT.example: The API validates the JWT before returning user data.

K8S.description: An open-source platform for running and managing containerized applications.
K8S.example: The service is deployed to the K8S cluster.

KPI.description: A metric used to measure progress toward a business or product goal.
KPI.example: Activation rate is the KPI for this onboarding project.

KYC.description: A process for verifying customer identity, often for compliance.
KYC.example: The payments flow requires KYC before payouts.

L10N.description: Adapting software or content for a specific language or region.
L10N.example: L10N is needed for the German launch.

LAN.description: A network that connects devices in a limited physical area.
LAN.example: The printer is only reachable on the office LAN.

LDAP.description: A protocol for accessing directory services such as users and groups.
LDAP.example: The admin portal syncs groups from LDAP.

LGTM.description: Used in code review or chat to indicate approval.

LFS.description: A Git extension used to manage large files outside normal Git objects.
LFS.example: Move the design exports to Git LFS.

LLD.description: A detailed technical design for implementation-level behavior.
LLD.example: The LLD explains the retry state machine.

LTV.description: The estimated value a customer brings over their relationship with a business.
LTV.example: The growth team compares LTV against CAC.

MAU.description: The number of unique users active during a month.
MAU.example: MAU increased after the mobile redesign.

MDM.description: Tools and policies used to manage company devices.

MFA.description: Authentication that requires multiple verification factors.
MFA.example: MFA is required for admin accounts.

MR.description: A request to merge code changes, commonly used in GitLab workflows.
MR.example: Please add screenshots to the MR description.

MRR.description: Recurring revenue normalized to a monthly value.
MRR.example: MRR grew after annual plans converted.

MTTA.description: The average time it takes to acknowledge an alert or incident.
MTTA.example: Reducing MTTA is part of the on-call improvement plan.

MTTD.description: The average time it takes to detect a problem or incident.

MTTR.description: The average time it takes to restore service after an incident.
MTTR.example: The postmortem includes MTTR for the outage.

MVP.description: The smallest useful version of a product that can validate an idea.
MVP.example: The MVP supports exact acronym lookup only.

NACK.description: Used to reject or disagree with a request, proposal, or message.
NACK.example: NACK on releasing today until the rollback plan is ready.

NPS.description: A customer loyalty metric based on how likely users are to recommend a product.
NPS.example: NPS improved after the support workflow changes.

OKR.description: A goal-setting framework with qualitative objectives and measurable results.
OKR.example: The team OKR is to reduce onboarding time.

OOO.description: Used when someone is unavailable because they are away from work.

OIDC.description: An identity layer on top of OAuth 2.0 used for sign-in.
OIDC.example: The app uses OIDC for SSO.

OAUTH.description: An authorization framework used to grant limited access without sharing passwords.
OAUTH.example: The integration uses OAuth to connect to the user's calendar.

OS.description: System software that manages hardware and applications.
OS.example: The issue only reproduces on the latest OS.

ORM.description: A technique or library that maps application objects to database tables.
ORM.example: The ORM migration adds a new users table.

P0.description: The highest priority level, usually reserved for urgent production issues.
P0.example: Treat the checkout outage as P0.

P1.description: A high priority issue that should be handled quickly.
P1.example: The login regression is a P1 bug.

P95.description: A metric value that 95 percent of observations are at or below.
P95.example: Track P95 latency for the search API.

P99.description: A metric value that 99 percent of observations are at or below.
P99.example: P99 latency spiked during the batch import.

PCI.description: Common shorthand for payment card security standards and compliance.
PCI.example: Do not log card numbers because of PCI requirements.

PII.description: Information that can identify a specific person.

PM.description: A person responsible for product direction, requirements, and tradeoffs.
PM.example: Ask the PM to confirm the launch scope.

PMF.description: A state where a product strongly meets market demand.
PMF.example: Retention is improving, but PMF is not proven yet.

POC.description: A small implementation used to prove an idea is technically possible.
POC.example: Build a POC before committing to the integration.

PR.description: A request to review and merge code changes into another branch.
PR.example: Open a PR once the tests pass.

PRD.description: A document that describes product goals, requirements, and expected behavior.
PRD.example: The PRD defines the first version of the search experience.

PTO.example: I am taking PTO next Monday.

QA.description: The process of checking that software meets quality expectations.
QA.example: QA found a regression in the checkout flow.

RACI.description: A responsibility matrix used to clarify ownership across roles.
RACI.example: Add a RACI table to the migration plan.

RAM.description: Fast working memory used by a computer while programs are running.
RAM.example: The build machine needs more RAM.

RBAC.description: A permissions model based on roles assigned to users or services.
RBAC.example: Use RBAC to restrict admin-only actions.

RCA.description: An investigation into the underlying cause of an incident or problem.
RCA.example: We will share the RCA after the incident review.

RDBMS.description: Database software that stores data in related tables.
RDBMS.example: PostgreSQL is the RDBMS for this service.

REST.description: An architectural style often used for web APIs.
REST.example: The service exposes a REST API for account lookup.

RFC.description: A document shared to gather feedback on a proposal or design.
RFC.example: Please review the RFC before Friday.

ROI.description: A measure of value gained compared with the cost of an investment.
ROI.example: The ROI is unclear unless we automate the manual review step.

RPC.description: A pattern where one program calls a procedure on another system.
RPC.example: The frontend talks to the backend through RPC calls.

RPO.description: The maximum acceptable amount of data loss after a failure.
RPO.example: The database backup plan targets a one-hour RPO.

RTO.description: The target time for restoring service after a failure.

S3.example: Upload the export file to S3.

SAAS.description: Software delivered as a hosted subscription service.
SAAS.example: The billing model is standard SaaS.

SAML.description: A standard for exchanging authentication and authorization data.
SAML.example: The enterprise customer requires SAML SSO.

SCM.description: Tools and practices for tracking source code changes.

SDLC.description: The process of planning, building, testing, releasing, and maintaining software.
SDLC.example: Security review happens early in the SDLC.

SDK.description: A collection of tools, libraries, or documentation for building with a platform.
SDK.example: Install the iOS SDK before building the sample app.

SEO.description: Improving content and pages so search engines can discover and rank them.
SEO.example: The landing page needs SEO metadata.

SEV.description: A level used to classify the impact of an incident or bug.
SEV.example: The incident was downgraded from SEV1 to SEV2.

SFTP.description: A secure protocol for transferring files.
SFTP.example: The vendor sends reports through SFTP.

SLA.description: A formal commitment about expected service performance or support.
SLA.example: The enterprise SLA requires a response within one hour.

SLI.description: A metric used to measure service reliability or performance.
SLI.example: Request latency is one of our SLIs.

SLO.description: A target value for a service reliability or performance metric.
SLO.example: The API has a 99.9% availability SLO.

SOC2.description: A security and compliance audit framework commonly requested by enterprise customers.
SOC2.example: The procurement review asks for our SOC2 report.

SOW.description: A document that defines project scope, deliverables, timing, and responsibilities.
SOW.example: The implementation cannot start until the SOW is signed.

SPA.description: A web app that updates one page dynamically instead of loading new pages.
SPA.example: The dashboard is implemented as a SPA.

SQL.description: A language used to query and manage relational databases.
SQL.example: Use SQL to inspect the failed import records.

SRE.description: An engineering discipline focused on reliable, scalable systems.
SRE.example: Ask SRE to review the alert thresholds.

SSH.description: A protocol for securely connecting to remote systems.
SSH.example: Use SSH to access the bastion host.

SSL.description: An older term often used casually for TLS certificates.
SSL.example: Renew the SSL certificate before it expires.

SSO.description: An authentication setup where one login gives access to multiple systems.

TBD.description: Used when a decision has not been made yet.

TOS.description: Rules and conditions that govern use of a product or service.
TOS.example: Legal updated the TOS before the public launch.

TCO.description: The full cost of owning and operating a system or product.
TCO.example: Compare TCO before choosing the database vendor.

TCP.description: A core internet protocol that provides reliable ordered data transfer.
TCP.example: The service listens on a TCP port.

TDD.description: A development practice where tests are written before implementation code.
TDD.example: Use TDD for the new parser behavior.

TLS.description: A protocol used to encrypt network communication.
TLS.example: The service requires TLS for all external traffic.

TL;DR.description: A short summary of a longer message or document.
TL;DR.example: Add a TL;DR at the top of the design doc.

TLDR.description: A short summary of a longer message or document.
TLDR.example: Add a TLDR at the top of the design doc.

TTL.description: A duration that controls how long data, cache entries, or DNS records remain valid.
TTL.example: Lower the DNS TTL before migration.

UAT.description: Testing by users or stakeholders to confirm a feature meets expectations.
UAT.example: UAT starts after QA signs off.

UI.description: The part of a product that users see and interact with.
UI.example: The UI should show a clear empty state.

UDP.description: A network protocol that sends datagrams without connection setup.
UDP.example: The metrics collector receives UDP packets.

URI.description: A string that identifies a resource by name, location, or both.
URI.example: The callback URI must match the OAuth app settings.

URL.description: A web address that identifies where a resource is located.
URL.example: Share the staging URL in the release channel.

UX.description: The overall experience a person has while using a product.
UX.example: The UX should make the next action obvious.

VPN.description: A secure network connection used to access private systems.
VPN.example: Connect to the VPN before accessing staging logs.

VPC.description: An isolated cloud network for running private resources.
VPC.example: The database runs inside the VPC.

WCAG.description: Guidelines for making web content accessible.
WCAG.example: The new color palette should meet WCAG contrast requirements.

WFH.description: Used when someone is working remotely from home.

WIP.description: Used when something is not finished yet.
WIP.example: Mark the PR as WIP until the migration is ready.

XML.description: A markup language for structured documents and data.
XML.example: The legacy integration still sends XML payloads.

XSS.description: A web security vulnerability where untrusted scripts run in a user's browser.
XSS.example: Escape user input to prevent XSS.

YAML.description: A human-readable data serialization format often used for configuration.
YAML.example: The CI workflow is configured in YAML.
